title: [phase:critique] [family:backend-fit] [provider_hint:codex] Return one concise line with your initial critique of using Google AI agent tooling as the main backend for Cursorvers.Inc. Findings first, one or two sentences only.
summary_text: Safest approach is a thin seam, not full refactor: - Avoid testing by importing [index.ts](/Users/masayuki/Dev/cursorvers_line_free_dev/supabase/functions/line-webhook/index.ts) directly because it has top-level runtime side effects (`createClient`, `Deno.serve`) and is hard to isolate. - Extract only prompt/risk routing branches (tool-mode + `polish:`/`check:` prefix paths currently around [index.ts:900](/Users/masayuki/Dev/cursorvers_line_free_dev/supabase/functions/line-webhook/index.ts:900) and [index.ts:973](/Users/masayuki/Dev/cursorvers_line_free_dev/supabase/functions/line-webhook/index.ts:973)) into one small exported function with injected deps (`handlePromptPolisher`, `handleRiskChecker`, `clearUserState`). - Keep handlers unchanged; dispatcher seam should only decide `handled?` and forward `rawInput` exactly, so behavior stays identical and testable. - In seam tests, assert routing precedence and payload: tool-mode wins over prefix, `check:`/`polish:` trimming is correct, empty suffix is forwarded (so handler-level blank-input UX still applies). - Keep one lightweight HTTP smoke test for wiring, but put most coverage in seam tests to avoid brittle network/signature test setup.
